items.passage:
Choice architecture refers to the deliberate design of decision-making environments that shape how consumers select among options. Pioneered by economists Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein, the framework holds that subtle features of presentation — the position of products on a shelf, the default option on a registration form, the order of items on a menu — exert powerful influence over choices that consumers experience as fully autonomous. [■]

This influence operates largely through cognitive defaults. When a particular outcome is pre-selected, consumers tend to retain it rather than override the choice with active deliberation. [■] Researchers describe this tendency as the status quo bias, and they have documented it across markets ranging from organ donation registries to retirement plan enrollment. Retailers exploit this pattern by positioning higher-margin products at eye level and lower-margin products on the bottom shelf, where searching out the better deal demands deliberate effort that most shoppers will not invest. [■]

However, choice architecture faces serious objections in practice. Critics argue that steering consumers toward particular options — even when framed as helpful — raises ethical concerns about transparency and consumer autonomy. [■] Furthermore, the same design techniques that nudge people toward retirement savings can equally promote excessive consumption, suggesting that the underlying methodology may carry no inherent moral direction.
paragraphs: Choice architecture refers to the deliberate design of decision-making environments that shape how consumers select among options. Pioneered by economists Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein, the framework holds that subtle features of presentation — the position of products on a shelf, the default option on a registration form, the order of items on a menu — exert powerful influence over choices that consumers experience as fully autonomous.

This influence operates largely through cognitive defaults. When a particular outcome is pre-selected, consumers tend to retain it rather than override the choice with active deliberation. Researchers describe this tendency as the status quo bias, and they have documented it across markets ranging from organ donation registries to retirement plan enrollment. Retailers exploit this pattern by positioning higher-margin products at eye level and lower-margin products on the bottom shelf, where searching out the better deal demands deliberate effort that most shoppers will not invest.

However, choice architecture faces serious objections in practice. Critics argue that steering consumers toward particular options — even when framed as helpful — raises ethical concerns about transparency and consumer autonomy. Furthermore, the same design techniques that nudge people toward retirement savings can equally promote excessive consumption, suggesting that the underlying methodology may carry no inherent moral direction.

questions.stem: The word 'exert' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: apply
options.B: resist
options.C: observe
options.D: require

questions.explanation: In context, choice-architecture features 'exert powerful influence' over consumer choices — that is, they apply or bring force to bear, which matches 'apply.'

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why do retailers place higher-margin products at eye level?

options.A: Because eye-level products are easier to keep clean and dust-free
options.B: Because consumers feel more attached to products at their direct sightline
options.C: Because most shoppers will not deliberately search for better deals
options.D: Because eye-level shelving allows products to be restocked more quickly

questions.explanation: The passage states that searching out the better deal demands deliberate effort 'that most shoppers will not invest,' so eye-level placement exploits this reluctance.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the status quo bias?

options.A: It develops only when consumers have substantial product expertise
options.B: It affects men and women in fundamentally different ways
options.C: It influences major life decisions beyond ordinary retail choices
options.D: It disappears when consumers have unlimited time for evaluation

questions.explanation: The passage cites organ donation registries and retirement plan enrollment as examples, indicating the bias extends beyond ordinary retail decisions into major life choices.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention retirement savings in paragraph 3?

options.A: To prove that choice architecture has been embraced by all governments
options.B: To trace the historical development of behavioral economics as a field
options.C: To compare retirement systems used in different industrialized economies
options.D: To illustrate that the same design techniques can serve opposite social aims

questions.explanation: The author contrasts nudging toward retirement savings with promoting excessive consumption to show that choice architecture itself is morally neutral and can serve opposing ends.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, when an online retailer pre-selects a one-month subscription rather than a pay-per-use option, the framing alone often determines which plan most customers ultimately purchase.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: Slot 2 directly follows the sentence about pre-selected outcomes being retained rather than overridden — the subscription example concretely illustrates that default-retention claim.

items.passage:
Intrinsic motivation refers to the drive to engage in an activity for its own inherent satisfaction rather than for external reward. Psychologist Edward Deci first documented the concept in the 1970s through experiments showing that paying participants to solve puzzles they had previously found enjoyable reduced their willingness to attempt similar puzzles later. The finding contradicted the prevailing assumption that adding rewards reliably strengthens engagement. [■]

This counterintuitive result reflects what researchers call the overjustification effect. When external incentives are introduced, individuals often reinterpret their behavior as performed to obtain the reward, eclipsing their original internal motivation. [■] Subsequent studies in classrooms have replicated the pattern: students given grades for activities they once pursued out of curiosity gradually disengage when the grading is removed. [■]

However, the relationship between rewards and motivation is not uniformly destructive. Unexpected rewards delivered after task completion, as well as feedback emphasizing competence rather than control, generally preserve and may even strengthen intrinsic interest. [■] These findings suggest that the design of recognition systems matters as much as the presence of recognition itself, a conclusion educators continue to grapple with when structuring incentives.
paragraphs: Intrinsic motivation refers to the drive to engage in an activity for its own inherent satisfaction rather than for external reward. Psychologist Edward Deci first documented the concept in the 1970s through experiments showing that paying participants to solve puzzles they had previously found enjoyable reduced their willingness to attempt similar puzzles later. The finding contradicted the prevailing assumption that adding rewards reliably strengthens engagement.

This counterintuitive result reflects what researchers call the overjustification effect. When external incentives are introduced, individuals often reinterpret their behavior as performed to obtain the reward, eclipsing their original internal motivation. Subsequent studies in classrooms have replicated the pattern: students given grades for activities they once pursued out of curiosity gradually disengage when the grading is removed.

However, the relationship between rewards and motivation is not uniformly destructive. Unexpected rewards delivered after task completion, as well as feedback emphasizing competence rather than control, generally preserve and may even strengthen intrinsic interest. These findings suggest that the design of recognition systems matters as much as the presence of recognition itself, a conclusion educators continue to grapple with when structuring incentives.

questions.stem: The word 'eclipsing' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: complementing
options.B: overshadowing
options.C: accelerating
options.D: clarifying

questions.explanation: In context, external incentives reinterpret behavior so that internal motivation is hidden or dominated by the reward — 'overshadowing' captures this best.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what did Deci's puzzle experiments demonstrate?

options.A: Rewarding participants for previously enjoyable puzzles reduced their later interest
options.B: Rewarding participants improved their problem-solving speed on subsequent puzzles
options.C: Rewarding participants enhanced their long-term memory for puzzle strategies
options.D: Rewarding participants encouraged broader collaboration on similar puzzle tasks

questions.explanation: The passage states that paying participants for puzzles they had previously found enjoyable 'reduced their willingness to attempt similar puzzles later.'

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Deci first documented the concept in experiments during the 1970s
options.B: Classroom studies have replicated the overjustification effect
options.C: Unexpected post-completion rewards can preserve intrinsic interest
options.D: The overjustification effect occurs equally with all forms of feedback

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 distinguishes feedback emphasizing competence (which preserves interest) from control-oriented feedback, so the effect does not occur equally across all feedback forms.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about students described in paragraph 2?

options.A: Their original engagement depended on the absence of teacher supervision
options.B: They generally enjoy puzzles more than other classroom assignments
options.C: Their original interest depended partly on the absence of grades
options.D: Their teachers explicitly trained them to ignore external rewards

questions.explanation: The passage notes that students 'once pursued' activities 'out of curiosity' but disengaged once grading was introduced — implying their initial interest was partly contingent on the lack of grades.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a teacher's brief written note praising the originality of a student's essay typically reinforces effort more effectively than awarding a graded score.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: Slot 4 directly follows the claim that competence-emphasizing feedback preserves intrinsic interest — the example of a teacher's written praise concretely illustrates such competence-focused feedback.

items.passage:
Polynesian navigators were the practitioners of an oral seafaring tradition that enabled long-distance voyages across the Pacific Ocean centuries before European exploration. Operating without writing, compasses, or mechanical instruments, navigators in places like Tahiti and the Marquesas committed vast bodies of empirical knowledge to memory and song. Their methods drew on stars, ocean swells, cloud formations, and the flight patterns of seabirds — observations chained together into systems sophisticated enough to locate islands across thousands of kilometers of open water. [■]

The training required to absorb this body of knowledge was rigorous and selective. [■] Apprentice navigators began their education in early adolescence and could expect a decade or more of instruction before sailing as the principal wayfinder. Senior practitioners passed on celestial sequences, swell patterns specific to particular routes, and the subtle visual cues that indicated approaching landfall. [■]

This tradition eroded during the colonial period as introduced navigation instruments displaced indigenous techniques, and many island communities lost their last working wayfinders. [■] However, recent revival projects have partially reconstructed the knowledge using collaborations between elder practitioners and academic researchers, suggesting that traditional methods may yet survive as a living rather than purely historical practice.
paragraphs: Polynesian navigators were the practitioners of an oral seafaring tradition that enabled long-distance voyages across the Pacific Ocean centuries before European exploration. Operating without writing, compasses, or mechanical instruments, navigators in places like Tahiti and the Marquesas committed vast bodies of empirical knowledge to memory and song. Their methods drew on stars, ocean swells, cloud formations, and the flight patterns of seabirds — observations chained together into systems sophisticated enough to locate islands across thousands of kilometers of open water.

The training required to absorb this body of knowledge was rigorous and selective. Apprentice navigators began their education in early adolescence and could expect a decade or more of instruction before sailing as the principal wayfinder. Senior practitioners passed on celestial sequences, swell patterns specific to particular routes, and the subtle visual cues that indicated approaching landfall.

This tradition eroded during the colonial period as introduced navigation instruments displaced indigenous techniques, and many island communities lost their last working wayfinders. However, recent revival projects have partially reconstructed the knowledge using collaborations between elder practitioners and academic researchers, suggesting that traditional methods may yet survive as a living rather than purely historical practice.

questions.stem: The word 'rigorous' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: ancient
options.B: communal
options.C: ceremonial
options.D: demanding

questions.explanation: The passage pairs 'rigorous' with 'selective' and notes 'a decade or more of instruction,' both of which align with 'demanding.'

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what did Polynesian navigators rely on to locate distant islands?

options.A: Magnetic compasses calibrated against local stars
options.B: Observations of stars, ocean swells, cloud formations, and seabird flight patterns
options.C: Detailed paper charts developed through generations of voyaging
options.D: Mathematical calculations of latitude using fixed daylight tables

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 explicitly lists 'stars, ocean swells, cloud formations, and the flight patterns of seabirds' as the navigators' methods.

questions.stem: What does 'observations' refer to in paragraph 1?

options.A: The stars, swells, cloud formations, and seabird flight patterns
options.B: The vast bodies of empirical memorized knowledge from instruction
options.C: The trained navigators operating without compasses or instruments
options.D: The long-distance voyages across the Pacific Ocean

questions.explanation: The dash in paragraph 1 introduces 'observations' as a renaming of the immediately preceding list of natural cues — stars, swells, cloud formations, and seabird flight patterns.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the navigation training described in paragraph 2?

options.A: It required participants to memorize lengthy written documents quickly
options.B: It eventually became open to anyone who chose to apply
options.C: It demanded intellectual ability and many years of commitment
options.D: It produced navigators who relied primarily on improvisation at sea

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes training as 'rigorous and selective' lasting 'a decade or more,' implying both intellectual demands and substantial commitment.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, students who showed unreliable memory for star sequences were dismissed from training before sailing on their first major voyage.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: Slot 2 directly follows the claim that training was 'rigorous and selective' — the example of dismissing students with unreliable memory concretely illustrates that selectivity.

items.passage:
The evolution of jaws transformed early vertebrate animals from passive filter feeders into active predators capable of pursuing larger and more diverse prey. The earliest fish, which lived during the Ordovician period roughly 480 million years ago, lacked jaws entirely, drawing food into a circular sucking mouth by means of muscular pumping. These primitive forms — known collectively as agnathans — were restricted to soft sediments and small particles that could be consumed without biting or tearing.

A transitional phase emerged in the Silurian period, when certain fish lineages developed modified gill arches that gradually shifted forward and downward. This anatomical reorganization produced rudimentary biting structures still hinged at the rear of the skull, though early jawed fish remained relatively small and ecologically modest. Over millions of years, these structures grew sturdier, allowing their bearers to grip, crush, or shear food rather than merely suck it in.

By the Devonian period, jawed fish had radiated into an extraordinary diversity of forms, from heavily armored placoderms to early sharks. These groups occupied predatory niches throughout the seas, and their feeding success drove rapid evolutionary expansion among other vertebrates as well. However, several armored lineages were lost during later mass extinctions, leaving sharks and bony fish to dominate subsequent vertebrate predation.
paragraphs: The evolution of jaws transformed early vertebrate animals from passive filter feeders into active predators capable of pursuing larger and more diverse prey. The earliest fish, which lived during the Ordovician period roughly 480 million years ago, lacked jaws entirely, drawing food into a circular sucking mouth by means of muscular pumping. These primitive forms — known collectively as agnathans — were restricted to soft sediments and small particles that could be consumed without biting or tearing.

A transitional phase emerged in the Silurian period, when certain fish lineages developed modified gill arches that gradually shifted forward and downward. This anatomical reorganization produced rudimentary biting structures still hinged at the rear of the skull, though early jawed fish remained relatively small and ecologically modest. Over millions of years, these structures grew sturdier, allowing their bearers to grip, crush, or shear food rather than merely suck it in.

By the Devonian period, jawed fish had radiated into an extraordinary diversity of forms, from heavily armored placoderms to early sharks. These groups occupied predatory niches throughout the seas, and their feeding success drove rapid evolutionary expansion among other vertebrates as well. However, several armored lineages were lost during later mass extinctions, leaving sharks and bony fish to dominate subsequent vertebrate predation.

questions.stem: The word 'shear' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: swallow
options.B: cut
options.C: soften
options.D: preserve

questions.explanation: The passage lists 'grip, crush, or shear food' as forms of active processing — 'shear' parallels physical cutting motion.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how did the earliest fish obtain food?

options.A: They captured prey by using their sharp pointed teeth and hooks
options.B: They chased small invertebrates across the shallow coral reef floor
options.C: They tore food from underwater rocks using bony armor plates and ridges
options.D: They drew food in by muscular pumping of a sucking mouth

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that the earliest fish drew food into 'a circular sucking mouth by means of muscular pumping.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the early jawed fish described in paragraph 2?

options.A: They were not yet capable of competing with the largest predators
options.B: They originated in freshwater habitats before invading the open ocean
options.C: They had retained the sucking mouth alongside their developing biting structures
options.D: They had quickly driven all earlier agnathan species to extinction

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes early jawed fish as 'relatively small and ecologically modest,' implying they could not yet compete with the largest contemporary predators.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention the Silurian period in paragraph 2?

options.A: To explain why all jawed fish later went extinct globally
options.B: To compare conditions there with those of the Ordovician period
options.C: To identify the moment when transitional jaw structures first emerged
options.D: To document the early appearance of sharks and bony fish

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 places the emergence of modified gill arches and rudimentary biting structures in the Silurian, identifying when the transitional jaw structures first arose.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Sharks evolved before other jawed fish during the Silurian period
options.B: Modified gill arches contributed to the development of vertebrate jaws
options.C: The early jawed fish remained ecologically modest in size
options.D: Placoderms were the heavily armored predators of the Devonian seas

questions.explanation: The passage places sharks in the Devonian radiation, not as the earliest Silurian jawed fish, so the statement in A contradicts the timeline.

items.passage:
Ion propulsion is a method of accelerating spacecraft by electrically charging atoms of a propellant gas and ejecting them at high speed. The technology was first proposed in the early twentieth century but did not enter operational use until decades later, when miniaturized power systems and improved control electronics made sustained ionization practical aboard small probes. Unlike conventional chemical rockets, ion engines produce very weak instantaneous thrust but can operate continuously for months or even years.

The engine works by feeding an inert propellant — typically xenon — into a chamber, where electrical fields strip electrons from each atom. The resulting positive ions are then accelerated through a series of charged grids and expelled at exhaust velocities roughly ten times greater than those of chemical engines. This high exhaust velocity translates into far greater fuel efficiency, allowing a probe to reach distant destinations using a fraction of the propellant a chemical rocket would require.

These advantages have made ion propulsion the preferred method for missions to small bodies and outer planets, where extended cruise times and minimal payload capacity favor incremental acceleration. However, the technology cannot replace chemical engines for launching spacecraft from Earth, where overwhelming initial thrust remains essential for escaping the planet's gravity.
paragraphs: Ion propulsion is a method of accelerating spacecraft by electrically charging atoms of a propellant gas and ejecting them at high speed. The technology was first proposed in the early twentieth century but did not enter operational use until decades later, when miniaturized power systems and improved control electronics made sustained ionization practical aboard small probes. Unlike conventional chemical rockets, ion engines produce very weak instantaneous thrust but can operate continuously for months or even years.

The engine works by feeding an inert propellant — typically xenon — into a chamber, where electrical fields strip electrons from each atom. The resulting positive ions are then accelerated through a series of charged grids and expelled at exhaust velocities roughly ten times greater than those of chemical engines. This high exhaust velocity translates into far greater fuel efficiency, allowing a probe to reach distant destinations using a fraction of the propellant a chemical rocket would require.

These advantages have made ion propulsion the preferred method for missions to small bodies and outer planets, where extended cruise times and minimal payload capacity favor incremental acceleration. However, the technology cannot replace chemical engines for launching spacecraft from Earth, where overwhelming initial thrust remains essential for escaping the planet's gravity.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The function, advantages, and limitations of ion propulsion in spacecraft
options.B: The historical development of xenon as a key propellant gas
options.C: The future replacement of chemical rockets in space missions
options.D: The role of electrical grids in modern spacecraft engineering

questions.explanation: The passage defines ion propulsion (paragraph 1), explains its operating mechanism (paragraph 2), and weighs its advantages against its limitations (paragraph 3).

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what propellant is typically used in ion engines?

options.A: Hydrogen
options.B: Xenon
options.C: Helium
options.D: Argon

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 specifies that the inert propellant fed into the chamber is 'typically xenon.'

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: Paragraph 2 challenges the historical claims made in paragraph 1's introduction
options.B: Paragraph 2 introduces an alternative competing with that in paragraph 1
options.C: Paragraph 2 explains the mechanism behind the technology of paragraph 1
options.D: Paragraph 2 surveys missions that have used the technology mentioned earlier

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 defines ion propulsion at a high level; paragraph 2 then explains how the engine physically operates, supplying the mechanistic detail.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about ion engines?

options.A: They were used during the earliest decades of space exploration
options.B: They consume more propellant than equivalent chemical rockets do
options.C: They require extensive in-flight refueling to complete deep-space missions
options.D: They were impractical for spacecraft use before reliable power systems existed

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 notes that ion propulsion did not enter operational use until miniaturized power systems and improved electronics made it practical — implying it was impractical earlier.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, why are ion engines preferred for missions to outer planets?

options.A: Because outer planets contain abundant supplies of xenon gas
options.B: Because chemical engines fail at extremely cold temperatures
options.C: Because extended cruise times favor sustained incremental acceleration
options.D: Because outer planets require unusually precise course corrections

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that 'extended cruise times and minimal payload capacity favor incremental acceleration,' which is the kind of thrust ion engines provide.
